artifacts.content:
Laura,

I wanted to get this all in one place while it’s still fresh in my head. I met with Megan Ruiz at the Elevations Credit Union branch in Idaho Springs this afternoon (10/14), and it’s basically what we were worried about. She said they cannot finalize the refinance until Clear Creek County confirms in writing that our 1.32‑acre parcel at 742 Evergreen Ridge Road is a legally created lot. She was nice about it, but firm – no exception, especially with the way interest rates have been bouncing around.

She also brought up timing. With your last paycheck from Alpine Orthopedics in Golden hitting at the end of September after they laid you off on 9/30/25, she’s looking at our income more closely. Megan said if we can get this wrapped up and closed by December 2025, we should be able to lock in the lower payment before our savings start getting uncomfortably low.

I didn’t tell her how much the job stuff on my side is stressing me out, but it’s in the back of my mind too, especially after Elena put me on that PIP at Rocky Mountain Renewables on 10/1. All the more reason to keep pushing on the county.

On the home front, Evan’s basketball schedule is up on the fridge, and Sophie’s piano lesson got moved to Thursdays at 4:30, so I’ll juggle drop‑offs on my work‑from‑home days. Let’s sit down after the kids are in bed tonight and make a list of who we need to call first thing tomorrow.

Love,
Michael

metadata.subject: Refi update after meeting with Megan

artifacts.content:
To‑do / brain dump – property + money stuff

– Call Clear Creek Planning tomorrow morning. Explain that Laura lost her project coordinator job at Alpine Orthopedics on 9/30/25 and that the refinance with Elevations Credit Union is stalled until they’re willing to say our 1.32 acres at 742 Evergreen Ridge is a legitimate single lot.

– Long‑term: keep Dad in mind. After his early‑stage Parkinson’s diagnosis at Poudre Valley Hospital in Fort Collins back in March 2024 (Dr. Karen Patel), we really need to stay on track for the idea of a small ADU over the detached garage by summer 2027 so he has a place here if living alone in that condo off Harmony Road stops being realistic.

– Revisit the HELOC idea with Wells Fargo in Denver once the lot status is cleaned up. Their underwriter, James McCoy, shot us down in August 2024 because the deed didn’t line up with the Mountain View Estates plat, and we still have around $32k hanging over us from Sophie’s emergency appendectomy at Children’s Hospital Colorado in Aurora in May 2023 plus the credit cards we floated it on.

– Honestly, I’m a little worried the county could eventually look closer and realize the way the property got carved up years ago probably didn’t follow the subdivision rules to the letter. Might be worth asking around about a land‑use attorney before I get too far down the road with calls and emails.

– Don’t forget normal life: schedule chimney sweep before heavy snow, order more pellets, and block off Saturdays for Evan’s games.

Hey Karen,

Thanks again for driving up on 7/12 and walking through the house with us. It helped a lot to see the comps you pulled for Idaho Springs and talk through realistic numbers instead of just guessing based on Zillow.

I’ve been thinking about what you said when we were sitting at the kitchen table going over that draft seller’s property disclosure. If we tried to list before Clear Creek cleans up the Lot 27 / remnant Lot 28 situation, you’d have to check the box and spell out that the house and septic system partly sit on an illegally created remnant. Hearing you say that could spook buyers and easily knock $25–40k off the sale price was a gut punch, but I’m glad you were honest.

Between that, Wells Fargo’s HELOC denial last August over the plat vs. deed mismatch, and now Elevations putting our refi on hold until the county confirms the lot is legal, it’s pretty obvious we can’t ignore this any longer.

I’m planning to call the Planning office this week and see what they recommend. Down the road, if we ever decide to sell off a piece instead of the whole 1.32 acres, I don’t want some title company blowing up the deal because they discover the old split was never properly blessed under county code.

On a lighter note, tell Daniel I finally fixed that slow leak in the garage roof he teased me about at Thanksgiving. And if you still have that roofer’s card you mentioned, send it along in case we get another surprise storm.

Love,
Michael

metadata.subject: Follow‑up on lot issue and possible sale down the road

artifacts.content:
Hey Dan,

Figured I’d give you the long version since you actually enjoy this property/finance stuff.

Short story: our little mountain dream parcel is more of a paperwork Frankenstein than we realized. Remember how we always talked about it as “our 1.32 acres on Evergreen Ridge”? Turns out the way the previous owners mashed together Lot 27 and a chunk of former Lot 28 back in the early ’90s was never fully cleaned up. The house and detached garage sit across what is technically still a lot line, and the septic leach field crosses it too.

This has come back to bite us three different ways now: (1) Wells Fargo’s underwriter, James McCoy, denied our HELOC in August 2024 when he saw the Mountain View Estates plat didn’t match our deed, which meant we couldn’t roll the ~$32k from Sophie’s emergency appendectomy at Children’s in Aurora (May 2023) into something more manageable; (2) Elevations Credit Union in Idaho Springs is currently refusing to finalize our refi until Clear Creek County confirms the 1.32‑acre parcel is legally created; and (3) Karen says if we list before it’s fixed, she has to disclose that the house and septic partly sit on an “illegally created remnant,” which could shave $25–40k off any offer.

On top of that, I got put on a formal PIP at Rocky Mountain Renewables on 10/1 after the Pueblo job slipped again. HR (Elena) basically said if my metrics don’t improve by January 2026, my Senior PM role could be in play. So we’re trying to get the county’s blessing and the refi locked while my income still looks stable.

Day‑to‑day life is otherwise normal chaos: Evan’s basketball, Sophie’s piano, me hauling wood and cursing at I‑70. Let’s grab a beer soon so you can tell me all the reasons I should’ve gone with a condo.

Michael

metadata.subject: Our cursed lot, in excruciating detail

artifacts.content:
Property history notes – for meeting with surveyor

– Current understanding: Our place at 742 Evergreen Ridge is effectively all of Tract B from that old unrecorded “Unofficial Boundary Line Adjustment for Thompson and Keller” survey (10/14/1993), in the NE 1/4 of the SE 1/4 of Section 14, Township 4 South, Range 73 West, 6th P.M. It’s basically Lot 27 of Mountain View Estates (about 0.89 acres) plus a remaining piece of former Lot 28 (about 0.43 acres) that got glommed on via a 1993 warranty deed.

– Legal description from the title packet: starting at the southeast corner of Lot 27, then N 15°23'10" W 210.45', then N 68°42'35" E 162.80' to Evergreen Ridge Road right‑of‑way, then S 21°17'25" E 198.60', then S 70°05'40" W 155.30' back to the beginning, total about 1.32 acres, plus that portion of former Lot 28 to the east.

– Talk through how the house (built 1995, CO issued 1996) and septic ended up straddling the old lot line. Make sure the new survey clearly shows the well, septic, detached garage, and driveway.

– Bring up long‑term plan: we’d like the option to build a small, code‑compliant ADU over the garage by summer 2027 so Dad has a place if his Parkinson’s progresses and he can’t stay in the Harmony Road condo.

– Background: when the previous owner “adjusted” things, it sounds like it was basically a handshake deal and some tape‑measure work, not a formal subdivision process. This is honestly the main reason to apply for an exemption application. I want the surveyor’s take on what it will take to get the county comfortable with how the land is actually being used.

– Also mention the practical fallout: HELOC denial in Aug 2024, refi hold with Elevations, and Karen’s warning about disclosures and lost value if we ever sell.

Event: Meeting with Dad’s neurologist – long‑term planning

Date/Time: 2024-03-28, 2:30–3:30 p.m.
Location: Poudre Valley Hospital, Neurology Clinic, Fort Collins
Attendees: Michael Thompson, Robert Thompson, Dr. Karen Patel

Notes:
– Purpose: follow‑up on Dad’s early‑stage Parkinson’s diagnosis from earlier in March 2024 and talk honestly about what the next 3–5 years could look like.
– Questions to ask Dr. Patel: realistic timeline for when living alone in his condo near Harmony Road might stop being safe; what kind of mobility issues to expect; whether stairs will become a big problem.
– Bring up idea of him eventually moving in with us in Idaho Springs. Mention that we’re looking at building a small accessory dwelling unit over the detached garage at 742 Evergreen Ridge Road by around summer 2027 so he can have his own space but still be close.
– Also explain the bureaucratic tangle: before we can even seriously design an ADU, Clear Creek County wants the existing 1.32‑acre parcel (Lot 27 + remnant Lot 28) recognized as a single, legally created lot. That’s currently holding up our refinance with Elevations Credit Union too, which we’re trying to get done while Laura is still looking for something new after the Alpine Orthopedics layoff.
– Logistical reminders: bring Dad’s med list, his PT notes, and a copy of the MRI report. Leave Idaho Springs by 12:15 to beat I‑25 traffic. Pick up Sophie from a friend’s house on the way back if it runs long.

metadata.title: Dad – Parkinson’s follow‑up & housing talk
metadata.location: Poudre Valley Hospital Neurology Clinic, Fort Collins

artifacts.content:
Hey Mom,

I’ve been meaning to sit down and give you a clearer picture of what’s going on with the house and money stuff, since I know you worry.

You already know about Sophie’s emergency appendectomy at Children’s Hospital Colorado in Aurora back in May 2023. Insurance covered a chunk, but between deductibles, follow‑ups, and the credit card we leaned on for gas and groceries during that stretch, we’re still carrying around $32,000 in combined medical and card debt. Last August (2024) we tried to set up a home equity line of credit at the Wells Fargo branch in downtown Denver to consolidate it, but their underwriter, a guy named James McCoy, noticed that the Mountain View Estates plat doesn’t match the legal description in our deed. He ended up declining the HELOC until Clear Creek County confirms our 1.32‑acre parcel is a properly created lot.

Fast‑forward to now: Elevations Credit Union in Idaho Springs is saying basically the same thing about the refinance. On top of that, Laura’s layoff from Alpine Orthopedics on September 30, 2025 means we really need that lower mortgage payment sooner rather than later.

I’m also juggling some stress at work – Rocky Mountain Renewables put me on a performance improvement plan on October 1 after a project in Pueblo slipped, and HR made it clear my Senior PM role could be at risk if things don’t improve by January 2026. So I’m trying to move all these pieces quickly while my income still looks solid on paper.

Day‑to‑day, the kids are good. Evan’s loving middle school and basketball, and Sophie is back to her usual chatterbox self, which is the best sign that the surgery is firmly in the rear‑view mirror for her.

Love,
Michael
